name: Morana
description: Morana, also known as Mara, Marzanna, or Marena, is a Slavic goddess associated with winter, death, harvest, and rebirth. Her origins are deeply rooted in ancient Slavic paganism, where she represented the inevitable decline and dormancy of nature during the cold winter months. Often depicted as a pale, icy figure, Morana embodies the forces of decay and the end of the agricultural cycle. While associated with death, Morana is not necessarily a malevolent figure. Instead, she represents the natural cycle of life, death, and renewal. Her symbolic death in effigy during spring festivals signifies the end of winter's reign and the promise of new life and abundance. She possesses power over the elements, particularly ice and snow, and is said to bring about harsh winters and barren lands. Morana’s role is crucial in the Slavic understanding of the cosmos; she is the necessary counterpart to the life-giving forces of spring and summer, ensuring the cyclical balance of nature. Her presence reminds people of the transient nature of existence and the importance of accepting both life and death. In some interpretations, she is also associated with nightmares and dark magic, reflecting the fears and uncertainties associated with the unknown.
subtitle: Goddess of Winter, Death, and Rebirth

keyMyths.title: The Burning of Morana
keyMyths.description: Each spring, an effigy of Morana is burned or drowned to symbolize the end of winter and the triumph of spring. This ritual ensures a bountiful harvest and the return of warmth and life.

keyMyths.title: Morana and the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Morana is sometimes viewed as a guardian of the underworld, guiding souls to their final resting place. She represents the transition from life to death and the mysteries that lie beyond.

keyMyths.title: Morana's Winter Reign
keyMyths.description: During the winter months, Morana holds sway over the land, bringing forth snow, ice, and harsh weather. Her power weakens as spring approaches, signaling her inevitable defeat.
